author: @GoogleMapsVids-Asia
text: I often wonder why a solar doesn’t convert water to hydrogen to use to make a small flame. Directed into a box from underneath the box full of fine sand. A hydrogen tiny torch can braze brass. So heat rises. The bottom of box has a copper cone. That goes into the box. The flame heats the copper that transfers to sand. The inside box is lined with clay and out side that radiant heat barrier then a metal case. Inside oil is routed to an external radiator and fan. I’d say box and system be housed in small shed. The box sand 4x4x4 brought to high temp like 1500 deg. Hopefully there be enough sun to create hydrogen. I figure 400 watts usually that’s about 4 amps. I’m betting your math skills can determine approximately how many watts or amps. I’ll go use AI see if I can figure it.

author: @DeclanMBrennan
text:
You cheer up a cloudy day and that's not just the LED Light. 🙂
2:30 "Generator - which is always some combination of wires and magnets"
That's got me wondering, could you have a piezo electric generator and perhaps have an elliptical gear that alternatively presses and releases the piezo electric crystal as the band unwinds?  Perhaps the resulting pulses could be smoothed out with a capacitor.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate and there are lots of ways to make a generator - I was perhaps a little loose and free with my terminology there lol

replies.author: @DistortedSemance
replies.text:
Yes, piezoelectric generators are a thing! They've been proposed for various purposes - usually low grade waste energy harvesting, from things like vibrating machinery or people's footsteps.

In this case, rather than a gear, my mind turned to a rotating eccentric mass, like the kind used in video game controllers. Planting the base of it against a piezo would create the necessary oscillating pressure without needing a bearing surface.

author: @totherarf
text:
I just had a flash back to something I did as a kid! With a rubber band! ;o)

You don't see them much nowadays but we used to make model planes out of Balsa wood and tissue. The tissue was coated with a polymer that dried hard to create a fairly robust(ish) skin for the plane. They were powered by a single elastic band run from the tail down the middle and hooked o tot he propeller! If you spent what seemed like ages winding the propeller when you released it the prop would spin and a casual toss in the air and your model was airborne ...... for quite some time!

OK, back to the here and now, rosy glow of happy childhood a mere memory ..... The key point was the length of time the prop would spin for! Maybe having a spring of rubber band a foot or so long might not be the best design choice here, but increasing the length would give a greater time generating power ..... if that is what you want!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Yep - i remember those too mate - like you made one myself lol - you could use a longer band - you could double them up you could use longer bands - so much you could do - i am looking forward to seeing what you come up with

replies.author: @NeilHoward
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering over wind it and the balsa that made the fuselage would bend & snap ! LOL

author: @bearnaff9387
text:
I feel that if you're going to keep going along this line of questioning and design, you might consider pulling in some of the work done by @lasersaber.  He has gotten some incredibly impressive results with variations on the old Joule Thief circuit.  His most recent video features a design that uses what appears to be a lawnmower pull-start to charge some supercapacitors.  The supercapacitors, running through his SJR Looper circuit, are able to take 30 seconds of repeated pulls and turn them into around an hour of illumination sufficient for a normal-sized living room (20m^2 or so).  

Hearing your threatening cackle of triumph again means more to me than you know.  I am so glad to see you back.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I love lasersaber mate but i think I did that last year in video 1871 - though i 3D printed everything the idea was exactly the same

author: @jamesross1003
text: Fun little project Robert.  Thanks so much for sharing.  What I think of with this setup is using gravity to wind and produce current at the same time.  Drop your weight and as it drops generate while winding.  Upon release the rubber band would continue to generate as it unwinds.  Rinse and repeat.  A flywheel would smooth out while you reset the weight.  Thanks again really liking these new projects/videos Robert!
